text: Cognitive scientists studying expertise have documented a phenomenon called 'inattentional blindness' — the failure to perceive unexpected stimuli when attention is narrowed by task-focus. Daniel Simons and Christopher Chabris made the effect famous with their 1999 gorilla experiment: subjects focused on counting basketball passes missed a figure in a gorilla suit walking through the scene. Zen beginner's mind addresses exactly this failure mode. The practitioner is trained not to narrow attention prematurely — to resist the efficiency of tunnel focus in favor of what the tradition calls 'wide-angle awareness.' The gorilla you miss is always the thing you didn't know you needed to see.
question: What did the Simons and Chabris gorilla experiment demonstrate, as described in the passage?

options.text: That focused attention improves recall of task-relevant information.
That wide-angle awareness is achievable only through Zen training.
That narrowed task-focus can cause people to miss entirely unexpected stimuli.
That inattentional blindness is a uniquely human perceptual limitation.
